normalized_obligations.lean_statement: def InOrder (t : BST α) : List α := ...
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the in-order traversal of the BST as a list of keys.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: def Subtree (t : BST α) (x : α) : Prop := x ∈ keys(t) ∧ (x is in the subtree rooted at some node of t)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Define what it means for a key to be in a subtree (or equivalently, define the set of keys contained in a subtree).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: def LowestBoth (t : BST α) (k1 k2 : α) : Prop := ∃ n, n is a node of t ∧ k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ ∀ m, m is a node on the path from root to n (excluding n) → ¬(k1 ∈ keys(subtree(m)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(m)))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the specification: the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys (i.e., closest to leaves among nodes containing both).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: def LCAKey (t : BST α) (k1 k2 : α) : α := ...
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Optionally define the key returned by the algorithm as the key stored at the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys (often equivalent to the LCA in a BST when both keys are present).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: (∀ x ∈ InOrder t, True) ∧ StrictAsc (InOrder t)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Assume the BST is well-formed in the sense that its in-order traversal is strictly ascending.
Assume both keys are present in the tree.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: def PathTo (t : BST α) (k : α) : List (Node α) := ...
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the unique search path from the root to the node containing key k in a BST.
From strict ascending in-order traversal, derive the BST ordering property (left subtree keys < node key < right subtree keys).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∀ k ∈ keys(t), PathTo t k ends at the unique node whose key is k
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: In a BST, searching for a present key reaches exactly the node containing that key.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∀ n, keys(subtree(left(n))) ⊆ {x | x < key(n)} ∧ keys(subtree(right(n))) ⊆ {x | x > key(n)}
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: State the ordering constraints on keys in left/right subtrees for any node.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∀ n, (k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n))) ↔ (n lies on both PathTo t k1 and PathTo t k2)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Characterize nodes whose subtrees contain both keys as exactly the common ancestors (or equivalently nodes on both search paths).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: BSTProperty t → ∃! n, n is a node of t ∧ k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ ∀ m on path root→n excluding n, ¬(k1 ∈ keys(subtree(m)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(m)))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove existence and uniqueness of the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: let r := rust_reference t k1 k2; LowestBoth t k1 k2 ∧ r = key(n) for the unique n satisfying LowestBoth t k1 k2
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Main correctness: the implementation returns the key stored at the unique lowest node whose subtree contains both keys.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: let r := rust_reference t k1 k2; (k1 ∈ keys(t) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(t) ∧ StrictAsc (InOrder t)) → (∃ n, n is node of t ∧ key(n)=r ∧ k1 ∈ keys(subtree(n)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(n)))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Soundness: the returned key corresponds to a node whose subtree contains both keys.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: let r := rust_reference t k1 k2; (k1 ∈ keys(t) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(t) ∧ StrictAsc (InOrder t)) → ∀ m, m is node on path root→node(r) excluding node(r) → ¬(k1 ∈ keys(subtree(m)) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(subtree(m)))
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Minimality: no proper ancestor of the returned node has both keys in its subtree.

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: rust_reference t k1 k2 terminates for all finite BSTs t
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: Prove the Rust implementation terminates (e.g., recursion/loop decreases along the search path).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: (k1 ∈ keys(t) ∧ k2 ∈ keys(t)) → ∃ r, rust_reference t k1 k2 = r
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: If both keys are present, the function returns some key (no failure/None case).

normalized_obligations.lean_statement: StrictAsc (InOrder t) → (k1 ≠ k2 → returned key is well-defined and unique)
normalized_obligations.nl_gloss: If keys are distinct, the returned key is uniquely determined by the lowest-both-node property.
